rationale: In the conceptual overview, Lee and Labroo (2004) is cited as key evidence that processing fluency affects consumer evaluations, and this supports the paper's hypotheses about fluency as a mediator. The authors explicitly say their mechanism "builds on prior work (e.g., Lee and Aaker 2004; Lee and Labroo 2004)" by showing a downstream consequence of fluency in efficacy and recycling outcomes. Although cited in a group, it is tied to a central explanatory pathway rather than a passing literature mention.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Lee and Labroo (2004) as part of the processing-fluency base and extends that line by testing fluency effects in conservation messaging. It proposes and shows that fluency from frame-construal match influences perceived self-efficacy and then recycling intentions/behaviors, beyond brand evaluation outcomes.
